EO.en: Almost always energized by people and expression; forms thoughts out loud, in real time; arranges life around interaction and can feel flat in prolonged quiet.

framing.en: Frame as energy, not attention-seeking — can still be a thoughtful listener.

EO.en: Thinks best in dialogue, seeks group settings, moves quickly from thought to speech, initiates easily; uses solitude briefly and functionally.
Speaks early in discussions, shares reactions readily, recharges through contact; long stretches alone can drain.
Often works out ideas by talking, gravitates to livelier settings, reaches out when the day has been too quiet; still values downtime.
Enjoys company and thinks a little better talking things through, but is equally at home alone; speaks up without a strong pull to be first.
Draws energy from both people and solitude, shifting by context; can lead a discussion or work quietly alone without either draining them.

framing.en: A real result — name the flexibility as a strength.

EO.en: Comfortable in company but recovers best with a little solitude, and tends to form a thought before voicing it; participates readily.
Prefers to think before speaking, keeps a reaction private until formed, needs some alone time to reset; engages selectively — depth over breadth.
Rehearses thoughts internally, speaks after others, solves problems alone before sharing; large gatherings are manageable but costly.
Processes almost entirely internally, guards new ideas until ready, protects solitary focus; at events gravitates to one quiet conversation.
Energy comes almost entirely from solitude and reflection; extended contact depletes and needs recovery; shares sparingly, only when fully formed.

framing.en: Separate from shyness — can be warm and capable, and simply runs on quiet.

IF.en: Attention goes to facts, specifics, and direct experience; starts a task without long deliberation; prefers proven step-by-step methods and takes things literally; drawn to traditional, established ways.

framing.en: Not a ceiling on ability — this is where practical mastery and reliability come from.

IF.en: Works from facts and clear detail, likes tactical tasks with a tangible result, wants precise instructions; reads things literally and trusts what has already worked.
Prefers precise questions and answers, focuses on doing over pondering, likes short concrete tasks; values facts over theories.
Leans practical and factual; comfortable with detail and proven methods, but will engage a new idea if its use is clear.
Trusts facts and direct experience a little more than theory, but moves comfortably between detail and the bigger picture.
Moves fluidly between concrete fact and abstract pattern, using whichever the situation calls for; can ground an idea in specifics and step back to see its meaning.

framing.en: A real result — name the range as an asset.

IF.en: Drawn slightly more to patterns and meaning than to raw detail, but keeps a practical footing and notices connections.
Fits new facts into a bigger idea, enjoys imagining how things could be, drawn to novel approaches — while still able to attend to detail.
Thinks in patterns and meaning, prefers strategic tasks and the big picture, works comfortably amid uncertainty; looks for a deeper sense in things.
Trusts ideas over particulars, prefers strategic work and keeps a clear vision of the whole, often bends given instructions toward a better way; drawn to the untested and new.
Lives in patterns, meanings, and what-could-be; explores ideas at length, wants freedom from fixed rules, and looks for deeper meaning everywhere; an inventor and reformer.

framing.en: Not impracticality as a flaw — this is where innovation and foresight come from; pairs well with concrete collaborators.

DB.en: Decides through argument, facts, and objective analysis, and follows the conclusion even at emotional cost; keeps a given word, takes on hard decisions, and does not retract a considered position.

framing.en: Not coldness — this is where clear, fair, defensible judgment comes from; the person can care deeply and still decide by logic.

DB.en: Wins points with facts and reasoning rather than persuasion, keeps assessments objective, organizes work well, and prefers to rely on themselves.
Argues from evidence, is proud of objective decisions, and will enter a useful disagreement when it serves the task.
Finds it easier to get results through arguments and facts than through emotions; assessments are mostly objective, with room for people's stakes.
Reasons toward decisions and can achieve more with facts than appeals, but readily adjusts when something clearly matters to someone.
Combines argument with feeling and weighs both the logic of a decision and its impact on people, choosing objectively or with care as the situation calls for.

framing.en: A real result — name the dual sensitivity as a strength.

DB.en: Considers impact on people a little before the cold calculation, but keeps the reasoning in view.
Reaches goals more through rapport and persuasion than debate; decisions often weigh the feelings of those affected.
Decisions are often values-based and considerate of others; strong at social communication and smoothing things over, and prefers agreement to conflict.
Leads with empathy and relationships, senses who needs support, and works to resolve tension even if it slows the task; flexible with commitments to keep goodwill.
Decides above all by people and values, seeking to honour everyone's feelings and keep relationships whole; avoids conflict and quickly yields a point to spare someone's feelings.

framing.en: Not irrationality — this is where empathy, loyalty, and cohesion come from; pairs with logic-led input on high-stakes technical calls.

SP.en: Keeps order in things, papers, and plans; seems to schedule life to the minute and is thrown by a broken plan; finishes what is started and is strongly punctual.

framing.en: Not rigidity as a fault — this is where reliability and dependable delivery come from.

SP.en: Plans work in advance and dislikes deviating from it, finishes the current task before starting the next, keeps a fixed routine, and is punctual.
Keeps order in things and tasks, plans ahead, does things now rather than later, and finishes before beginning something new.
Likes working to a plan because it lifts productivity, dislikes putting tasks off, and prefers finishing to starting.
Given the choice, leans toward order and planning over improvising, and prefers to arrive on time.
Comfortable with both order and spontaneity; can work to a plan or freely, likes starting and finishing about equally, and is usually on time.

framing.en: A real result — name the adaptability as a strength.

SP.en: Given the choice, leans toward free work over a fixed plan and isn't troubled by arriving a little late.
Dislikes working strictly to plan as it limits freedom, tends to postpone tasks, and prefers starting to finishing.
Doesn't plan far ahead, waiting to see what's really needed; often works at the last minute, and likes starting more than finishing.
A creative clutter in things and tasks, prefers doing what feels right in the moment, may run past deadlines waiting for a second wind, and juggles many open tasks.
Order gives way to spontaneity; lives in the moment and finds schedules confining, does things at the last minute, and often leaves tasks open as new ones appear.

framing.en: Not disorganization as a flaw — this is where improvisation and comfort with uncertainty come from; pairs with structure-minded support on long deadlines.

ER.en: Stays composed even in a crisis and keeps a level mood regardless of events; controls emotions and can work in almost any state — very hard to knock off balance.

framing.en: Not detachment or absence of feeling — this is where composure, resilience, and steadiness-for-others come from.

ER.en: Makes decisions without being swept up by emotion, needs a real reason for a mood to shift, and can work through poor physical or emotional states.
Stays calm and contained where others lose composure, with an even working capacity across the day.
Fairly calm in tense moments and generally emotionally steady, with occasional stronger reactions to what matters most.
Usually calm under pressure; mood doesn't swing much across the day and rarely drives performance.
Feels events with normal intensity and recovers at a normal pace — no major mood swings through the day, responsive without being derailed.

framing.en: A healthy, common middle — describe as emotionally typical and adaptable.

ER.en: Feels events a little more keenly and takes a bit longer to settle, without being easily overwhelmed.
Cannot stay entirely cool in tense moments; mood and working capacity shift somewhat through the day, and feelings run fairly deep.
Openly shows feeling under stress; mood can change several times a day, and works best on an emotional upswing.

framing.en: Describe as attunement and depth of feeling, not fragility.

ER.en: Visibly stirred in critical moments; mood shifts noticeably and carries working capacity with it, with strong peaks of inspiration.

framing.en: This is where empathy and expressive depth come from; benefits from reassurance and supportive structure.

ER.en: Responds to events with great intensity; mood can turn several times a day — highly effective on an upswing, drained in a dip — and feels things vividly and deeply.

framing.en: MOST wording-sensitive cell. Describe as high sensitivity and depth of feeling — NEVER instability or disorder. For a child: 'feels deeply, benefits from gentle support and reassurance.'
